MasterControl EDA

  • 1 Introduction
  • 2 Description of the data
  • 3 Discussion of missing data
    • 3.0.1 Converting blanks to NA
    • 3.0.2 Grouping unique values to larger categories
  • 4 Exploratory visualizations and /or summary tables
  • 5 Results

1 Introduction

MasterControl’s Manufacturing Solutions product (Mx) is significantly underperforming its Quality Solutions product (Qx). The company believes it may not be targeting the right companies or the right people within those companies, leading to missed revenue opportunities and wasted marketing expenditures.

Conversion predictions will be generated using a classification model. The model will use historical sale leads and data collected from MasterControl. Then we will analyze patterns produced by the models to identify similarities between higher-converting accounts.

The purpose of this EDA notebook is to explore the relationships between different variables in the data. We will explore questions like what are the common characteristics of contacts/companies that are best prospects for Mx and how they differ from Qx products.

2 Description of the data

# Import packages
suppressMessages(suppressWarnings({
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)
  }))

# Import csv file
master <- read.csv("Mastercontrol QAL Performance.csv")

# Quick look at the data
master |>
  str()
## 'data.frame':    16644 obs. of  14 variables:
##  $ acct.primary.site.function  : chr  "Food and beverage" "Surgical instruments" "Cell therapies (CAR-T, NK)" "In-vitro diagnostic (IVD) kits" ...
##  $ acct.manufacturing.model    : chr  "Consumer Packaged Goods" "In-House" "Hospital Investigational Drug Unit" "In-House" ...
##  $ acct.target.industry        : chr  "Non-Life Science" "Medical Device" "Blood & Biologics" "Medical Device" ...
##  $ contact.lead.title          : chr  "QA Manager" "Quality Manager" "ING" "Sr. Director of IT" ...
##  $ QAL.ID                      : chr  "a0dPQ000005WbfuYAC" "a0dPQ00000CRpkLYAT" "a0dPQ000000KWdFYAW" "a0dPQ000004LOWTYA4" ...
##  $ contact.lead.id             : chr  "0030c00002XPeNGAA1" "0030c00002ZaC9pAAF" "0030c00002ZzekAAAR" "0030c00002a0E0cAAE" ...
##  $ next_stage__c               : chr  "SQL" "Won" "Recycled" "Recycled" ...
##  $ Priority                    : chr  "P1 - Webinar Demo" "Priority 2" "Priority 2" "P1 - Webinar Demo" ...
##  $ acct.territory.rollup       : chr  "Americas" "Americas" "EMEA" "Americas" ...
##  $ acct.tier.rollup            : chr  "Medium" "Medium" "Large" "Medium" ...
##  $ solution                    : chr  "Mx" "Mx" "Qx" "Logbooks" ...
##  $ solution_rollup             : chr  "Mx" "Mx" "Qx" "Mx" ...
##  $ last.tactic.campaign.channel: chr  "Online Ads" "SEO" "Email" "External Demand Gen" ...
##  $ qal.cohort.date             : chr  "2025-06-16" "2025-11-13" "2024-02-15" "2025-04-29" ...
master |>
  summary()
##  acct.primary.site.function acct.manufacturing.model acct.target.industry
##  Length:16644               Length:16644             Length:16644        
##  Class :character           Class :character         Class :character    
##  Mode  :character           Mode  :character         Mode  :character    
##  contact.lead.title    QAL.ID          contact.lead.id    next_stage__c     
##  Length:16644       Length:16644       Length:16644       Length:16644      
##  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##    Priority         acct.territory.rollup acct.tier.rollup     solution        
##  Length:16644       Length:16644          Length:16644       Length:16644      
##  Class :character   Class :character      Class :character   Class :character  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character      Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##  solution_rollup    last.tactic.campaign.channel qal.cohort.date   
##  Length:16644       Length:16644                 Length:16644      
##  Class :character   Class :character             Class :character  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character             Mode  :character
# Checking the proportion of each target variable value
prop.table(table(master$next_stage__c))
## 
##                   Exclude          INQ         Lost          MQL     Recycled 
## 0.0236721942 0.0732396059 0.0001201634 0.0007209805 0.0006008171 0.7221220860 
##          SQL          SQO          Won 
## 0.1618601298 0.0152607546 0.0024032684

There are 16,815 observations with 14 variables that are all characters. If we add all of the observations that have progressed (SQL, SQO, and Won) then that makes up about 17.7% of the data.

Right now, qal.cohort.date is stored as characters, we will have to convert this to a date format.

master$qal.cohort.date <- ymd(master$qal.cohort.date)

3 Discussion of missing data

# Checking for blanks in each column
NA_col <- colSums(master == "", na.rm = TRUE)

# Check total blanks across all data
NA_total <- sum(master == "", na.rm = TRUE)

NA_col
##   acct.primary.site.function     acct.manufacturing.model 
##                           60                          101 
##         acct.target.industry           contact.lead.title 
##                           45                         4860 
##                       QAL.ID              contact.lead.id 
##                            0                            3 
##                next_stage__c                     Priority 
##                          394                            0 
##        acct.territory.rollup             acct.tier.rollup 
##                          132                           45 
##                     solution              solution_rollup 
##                            0                            0 
## last.tactic.campaign.channel              qal.cohort.date 
##                            0                            0
NA_total
## [1] 5640

There are 8 variables that have blank values, and a total of 5,640 cells that were left blank.

3.0.1 Converting blanks to NA

We have identified the blanks so now we can convert them to NA.

master_clean <- master |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~ na_if(., "")))

# Check for blanks again
colSums(master_clean == "", na.rm = TRUE)
##   acct.primary.site.function     acct.manufacturing.model 
##                            0                            0 
##         acct.target.industry           contact.lead.title 
##                            0                            0 
##                       QAL.ID              contact.lead.id 
##                            0                            0 
##                next_stage__c                     Priority 
##                            0                            0 
##        acct.territory.rollup             acct.tier.rollup 
##                            0                            0 
##                     solution              solution_rollup 
##                            0                            0 
## last.tactic.campaign.channel              qal.cohort.date 
##                            0                            0
# Calculating the percent of NAs in each column
master_clean |>
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   ~ round(mean(is.na(.)) * 100, 2)))
##   acct.primary.site.function acct.manufacturing.model acct.target.industry
## 1                       0.36                     0.61                 0.27
##   contact.lead.title QAL.ID contact.lead.id next_stage__c Priority
## 1              29.21      0            0.02          2.37        0
##   acct.territory.rollup acct.tier.rollup solution solution_rollup
## 1                  0.79             0.27        0               0
##   last.tactic.campaign.channel qal.cohort.date
## 1                            0               0

acct.primary.site.function, acct.manufacturing.model, acct.target.industry, contact.lead.id, next_stage__c, acct.territory.rollup, and acct.tier.rollup all have less than 3% NAs. We can delete these since they make up such a miniscule amount.

master_clean <- master_clean |>
  filter(!is.na(acct.primary.site.function),
         !is.na(acct.manufacturing.model),
         !is.na(acct.target.industry),
         !is.na(contact.lead.id),
         !is.na(next_stage__c),
         !is.na(acct.territory.rollup),
         !is.na(acct.tier.rollup))

# Checking to see if NAs were dropped in those columns
master_clean |>
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   ~ round(mean(is.na(.)) * 100, 2)))
##   acct.primary.site.function acct.manufacturing.model acct.target.industry
## 1                          0                        0                    0
##   contact.lead.title QAL.ID contact.lead.id next_stage__c Priority
## 1              28.88      0               0             0        0
##   acct.territory.rollup acct.tier.rollup solution solution_rollup
## 1                     0                0        0               0
##   last.tactic.campaign.channel qal.cohort.date
## 1                            0               0

contact.lead.title has 28.88% of its data NAs. Since this is such a large amount, we will keep these rows because they could still hold valuable information. There are no numeric values in the dataset so we can convert NA to “Missing” instead for categorical analysis.

master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(across(where(~ is.character(.)), 
                ~ ifelse(is.na(.), "Missing", as.character(.))))

# Checking NAs
master_clean |>
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   ~ round(mean(is.na(.)) * 100, 2)))
##   acct.primary.site.function acct.manufacturing.model acct.target.industry
## 1                          0                        0                    0
##   contact.lead.title QAL.ID contact.lead.id next_stage__c Priority
## 1                  0      0               0             0        0
##   acct.territory.rollup acct.tier.rollup solution solution_rollup
## 1                     0                0        0               0
##   last.tactic.campaign.channel qal.cohort.date
## 1                            0               0

Now there are no NAs in the dataset. They have been replaced by “Missing” instead and we will treat them as its own category.

3.0.2 Grouping unique values to larger categories

Another important thing about this dataset is that there are many unique values for each column. This creates high cardinality and can make it hard for us to model our data. We will group these categories into larger groups to make them more meaningful.

# Grouping SQL, SQO, and Won as Success and the rest as Failure
master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(success_binary = ifelse(
    next_stage__c %in% c("SQL", "SQO", "Won"),
    1,
    0
  ))

# Checking class balance
prop.table(table(master_clean$success_binary))
## 
##        0        1 
## 0.815193 0.184807
# Converting all contact lead titles to uppercase for standardization
master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(contact.lead.title = str_trim(contact.lead.title),
         contact.lead.title = str_to_upper(contact.lead.title))

# Grouping contact lead titles to more general categories
master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(title_group = case_when(
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "CHIEF|CEO|CFO|CTO|COO|CIO|PRESIDENT|OWNER|FOUNDER|EXECUTIVE|DIR") ~ "Executive",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "VP|VICE PRESIDENT|HEAD") ~ "VP / Head",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "DIRECTOR") ~ "Director",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "MANAGER|SUPERVISOR|MANAGE|MANGER") ~ "Manager",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "ENGINEER|SCIENTIST|TECHNICAL|DEVELOPER|LEAD|TECHNICIAN|ARCHITECT") ~ "Technical",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "ANALYST") ~ "Analyst",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "SPECIALIST|ASSOCIATE|COORDINATOR") ~ "Specialist",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "CONSULTANT|CONSULTING") ~ "Consultant",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "MISSING") ~ "Unknown",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "PROFESSOR|RESEARCHER|RESEARCH|LECTURER|TEACHER|PROF") ~ "Academia",
    str_detect(contact.lead.title, "SALES") ~ "Sales",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))

# Checking title group distribution
prop.table(table(master_clean$title_group))
## 
##    Academia     Analyst  Consultant   Executive     Manager       Other 
## 0.005230386 0.009962640 0.016749689 0.199688667 0.225404732 0.077957659 
##       Sales  Specialist   Technical     Unknown   VP / Head 
## 0.002366127 0.044209215 0.086052304 0.288792030 0.043586550
# Converting all primary site functions to uppercase for standardization
master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(acct.primary.site.function = str_trim(acct.primary.site.function),
         acct.primary.site.function = str_to_upper(acct.primary.site.function))

# Grouping acct primary site function
master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(site_function = case_when(
    str_detect(acct.primary.site.function, "OSD|STERILE INJECTABLES|API|BIOLOGICS|CELL THERAPIES") ~ "Active Pharma Manufacturing",
    str_detect(acct.primary.site.function, "IMPLANTABLE DEVICES|IVD|INSTRUMENTS|PHARMA|HEALTH|MEDICAL|SURGICAL|PATIENT|DRUG|CARE|DIAGNOSTIC|BLOOD|VACCINE") ~ "Medical Device Manufacturing",
    str_detect(acct.primary.site.function, "SERVICES|CONSULTING|NON-MANUFACTURING") ~ "Non-Manufacturing",
    str_detect(acct.primary.site.function, "FOOD|BEVERAGE|NUTRACEUTICAL|COSMETICS|CHEMICALS") ~ "Other Manufacturing",
    str_detect(acct.primary.site.function, "NOT ENOUGH INFO FOUND|MISSING") ~ "Unknown",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))

prop.table(table(master_clean$site_function))
## 
##  Active Pharma Manufacturing Medical Device Manufacturing 
##                   0.11811955                   0.19134496 
##            Non-Manufacturing                        Other 
##                   0.33100872                   0.14396015 
##          Other Manufacturing                      Unknown 
##                   0.06058531                   0.15498132

4 Exploratory visualizations and /or summary tables

table(master_clean$success_binary)
## 
##     0     1 
## 13092  2968
prop.table(table(master_clean$success_binary))
## 
##        0        1 
## 0.815193 0.184807
# Distinction between Mx and Qx successes
master_clean |>
  group_by(solution_rollup) |>
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    successes = sum(success_binary),
    success_rate = mean(success_binary)
  )
## # A tibble: 2 × 4
##   solution_rollup total successes success_rate
##   <chr>           <int>     <dbl>        <dbl>
## 1 Mx               4040       524        0.130
## 2 Qx              12020      2444        0.203

There are 2,968 recorded successes and 13,092 failures. Earlier we calculated that 17.7% of the dataset were successes but after cleaning the data, that has increased to 18.5%.

Within Mx solutions alone, we have a success rate of 12.9% while Qx has 20.3%, which is expected.

master_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(x = title_group, fill = factor(success_binary))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ solution_rollup) +
  labs(fill = "Success",
       x = "Title Group",
       y = "Proportion",
       title = "Success Rate by Title Group")

A lot of the contact lead titles were left blank so there are many Unknowns that make up a lot of the data. If we ignore that category though, we see that many of the successes are from Manager/Executive roles. The next best category are Technical roles.

master_clean |>
  group_by(site_function, solution_rollup) |>
  summarise(success_rate = mean(success_binary, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = site_function, y = success_rate, fill = solution_rollup)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(success_rate, accuracy = 1)), hjust = -0.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ solution_rollup) +
  labs(
    x = "Site Function",
    y = "Success Rate",
    title = "Success Rate by Site Function"
  )
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'site_function'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.

Much like the lead titles, there are also a lot of data that is missing and make up the majority of failures and successes in the dataset. If we ignore the Unknown category, the next biggest contributors are medical device manufacturing and active pharma site functions.

master_clean |>
  group_by(acct.target.industry, solution_rollup) |>
  summarise(success_rate = mean(success_binary, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = acct.target.industry, y = success_rate, fill = solution_rollup)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(success_rate, accuracy = 1)), hjust = -0.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ solution_rollup) +
  labs(
    x = "Target Industry",
    y = "Success Rate",
    title = "Success Rate by Target Industry"
  )

Most successes are seen within the pharma and biotech, non-life science, and medical device industries.

# Creating a month variable
master_clean <- master_clean |>
  mutate(month = floor_date(qal.cohort.date, "month"))

# Success rate by month filtered to only Mx products
master_clean |>
  filter(solution_rollup == "Mx") |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    success_rate = mean(success_binary)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = success_rate)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Mx Success Rate by Month",
       x = "Month",
       y = "Success Rate") +
  theme_minimal()

Mx success rate seem to hover between 15% - 25% between 2024 and 2025. It seems to have performed better in 2024 than it did in 2025, but not by a lot.

master_clean |> 
  filter(solution_rollup == "Mx") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = acct.territory.rollup, fill = factor(success_binary))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Mx Success Rate by Territory",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()

Mx products predominately show successes in Japan and the Americas, with lower presence in EMEA and APAC/Oceania markets.

5 Results

Our findings show that Mx product successes are most often achieved when the contact lead titles are at the executive or manager level within medical device manufacturing and active pharma site functions. The target industries for these customers are pharma and biotech, non-life science, and medical devices. Mx’s success rates are pretty stagnant in 2024 and 2025, with slightly lower performance in 2025. Lastly, the Mx product is most successful in Japan, the Americas, and EMEA territories.

Although our EDA highlights key trends within the data, there is still a substantial portion where there are missing/unknown values. This limits the insights we can draw from this and we will have to take this in consideration as we move forward into the modeling part of our project.
